lineage: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
Events at Edessa: Constancy of the Devout Citizens, and Courage of a Pious Woman.

ccel.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

title: Events at Edessa: Constancy of the Devout Citizens, and Courage of a Pious Woman.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But we must here mention certain circumstances that occurred at Edessa in Mesopotamia.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: There is in that city a magnificent church dedicated to St. Thomas the Apostle, wherein, on account of the sanctity of the place, religious assemblies are incessantly held.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The Emperor Valens wishing to inspect this edifice, and having learnt that all who usually congregated there were opposed to the heresy which he favored, he is said to have struck the prefect with his own hand, because he had neglected to expel them thence also.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: As the prefect after submitting to this ignominy, was most unwillingly constrained to subserve the emperor’s indignation against them,—for he did not desire to effect the slaughter of so great a number of persons,—he privately suggested that no one should be found there.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But no one gave heed either to his admonitions or to his menaces; for on the following day they all crowded to the church.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: And when the prefect was going towards it with a large military force in order to satisfy the emperor’s rage, a poor woman leading her own little child by the hand hurried hastily by, on her way to the church, breaking through the ranks of the prefect’s company of soldiers.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The prefect irritated at this, ordered her to be brought to him, and thus addressed her: ‘Wretched woman! whither are you running in so disorderly a manner?’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: She replied, ‘To the same place that others are hastening.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: ‘Have you not heard,’ said he, ‘that the prefect is about to put to death all that shall be found there?’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: ‘Yes,’ said the woman, ‘and therefore I hasten that I may be found there.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: ‘And whither are you dragging that little child?’ said the prefect: the woman answered, ‘That he also may be made worthy of martyrdom.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The prefect on hearing these things, conjecturing that a similar resolution actuated the others who were assembled there, immediately went back to the emperor, and informed him that all were ready to die in behalf of their own faith.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: He added that it would be preposterous to destroy so many persons at one time, and thus persuaded the emperor to control his wrath.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: In this way were the Edessenes preserved from being massacred by order of their sovereign.
